content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Last week in a group chat, I saw a frontend dev with a ponytail complaining: his monorepo took 45 seconds to run a single `pnpm run build`. 45 seconds isn't a long time, but running it dozens of times a day adds up to five full days a year just staring at a terminal spinner.
I replied: "Try Nub."
He asked: "Another new runtime?"
I said no. Then I sent him the install command. After trying it, he replied: "Holy shit, I didn't even have to change my code."
Why Your Node.js Is Getting Slower
Node.js's toolchain is held together by duct tape.
You need `node` to run files, `npm run` / `pnpm run` to run scripts, `npx` to run binaries, `tsx` to run TypeScript, `nodemon` for hot reload, `dotenv` to load environment variables, `nvm` to manage multiple versions...
Seven tools, each one starting a new Node.js process. Each startup has a 100–300ms "wrapping tax"—and that's the time you waste every day.
Bun and Deno saw this problem, but their solution was: replace the entire runtime. Your code has to be compatible with new APIs, your CI/CD has to swap images, and deployment platforms (AWS, GCP, Azure) still treat them as second-class citizens.
